 {"id":"bd-2kkn4.5","title":"Unit tests: rw-antidependency detection and FCW resolution correctness","description":"Focused unit tests for the SSI rw-antidependency cycle detection algorithm and first-committer-wins resolution. These are deterministic tests with hand-crafted conflict schedules that verify each component of SSI validation in isolation.

## Test Categories

1. No-conflict baseline: Two transactions with disjoint read-sets and write-sets. Verify both commit successfully with no SSI abort.

2. Read-write antidependency detection: T1 reads page P, T2 writes page P. Verify the rw-antidependency T1->T2 is correctly detected at T2 commit time.

3. Dangerous structure (bidirectional): T1 reads P1 and writes P2. T2 reads P2 and writes P1. Verify the dangerous structure (cycle T1->T2->T1) is detected and one transaction is aborted.

4. Classic write-skew (bank transfer): Two accounts with constraint sum >= 0. T1 reads both, decrements A. T2 reads both, decrements B. Verify exactly one commits and the constraint is preserved.

5. Doctor on-call: Three doctors, constraint at least one on-call. T1 removes self seeing others on-call. T2 removes self seeing others on-call. Verify exactly one commits.

6. FCW ordering: In a dangerous structure, verify the first-committing transaction always wins (later-committing one is aborted).

7. Deterministic tiebreaker: Two transactions with simultaneous commit. Verify the tiebreaker is deterministic (lower txn_id wins).

8. Safe snapshot shortcut: Transaction with a safe snapshot (no concurrent conflicting transactions). Verify SSI validation is skipped (optimization path).

9. Three-transaction cycle: T1->T2->T3->T1 rw-antidependency cycle. Verify at least one transaction is aborted to break the cycle.

10. Evidence ledger integration: Verify that every SSI abort produces an evidence ledger entry with correct conflict details.

## Alien CS Reference
Cahill, Rohm, Fekete 2009 Serializable Isolation for Snapshot Databases: Section 3 specifies the dangerous structure definition and detection algorithm. Ports, Grittner 2012 Serializable Snapshot Isolation in PostgreSQL: Section 3.2 describes the two-hop optimization for efficient cycle detection.

## Rust Implementation Guidance
- Implement each test with explicit transaction begin/read/write/commit sequencing (no threads needed for unit tests)
- Use a mock TransactionManager that allows deterministic control of commit ordering
- For evidence ledger verification: inject a test evidence ledger sink and assert on logged entries
- For FCW ordering: use explicit commit ordering via sequential commit calls
- Each test should be self-contained with its own database state

## Logging Requirements
- Log SSI validation decisions at tracing::debug level: txn_id, conflict_type, resolution
- Log evidence ledger entries at tracing::info level: abort_reason, conflicting_txns, pages_involved
- Log test assertions at tracing::debug level for debugging

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All 10 test categories implemented and passing
- [ ] Zero false negatives (no write-skew anomaly escapes detection)
- [ ] FCW ordering verified deterministic
- [ ] Evidence ledger entries verified for all aborts
- [ ] Safe snapshot optimization verified (no unnecessary validation)
- [ ] All tests deterministic (no timing dependence)
- [ ] All tests complete in < 2 seconds total","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T17:35:19.154147115Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-13T20:47:27.185292263Z","closed_at":"2026-02-13T20:47:27.185268839Z","close_reason":"Completed focused SSI/FCW unit-test coverage in crates/fsqlite-mvcc/src/ssi_validation.rs and crates/fsqlite-mvcc/src/begin_concurrent.rs.  {"id":"bd-2kvo","title":"§16 Phase 3: B-Tree Core (Interior/Leaf Nodes, Cursor, Insert/Delete/Search)","description":"## §16 Phase 3: B-Tree and SQL Parser

### Deliverables
- crates/fsqlite-btree/src/cursor.rs: BtCursor with page-stack traversal (max depth 20 for 4KB pages; with interior page fanout ~300-400 for table B-trees, capacity vastly exceeds any practical database size even at depth 5-6)
- crates/fsqlite-btree/src/cell.rs: Cell parsing for all 4 page types (INTKEY table leaf/interior, BLOBKEY index leaf/interior), overflow detection, local payload calculation
- crates/fsqlite-btree/src/balance.rs: Page splitting algorithms -- balance_nonroot (redistribute cells among siblings, typically 3-way split), balance_deeper (root overflow, increase tree depth by 1)
- crates/fsqlite-btree/src/overflow.rs: Overflow page chain read/write, following chain links, allocating overflow pages from freelist
- crates/fsqlite-btree/src/freelist.rs: Trunk + leaf freelist page management, allocation (prefer leaf pages from first trunk), deallocation
- crates/fsqlite-btree/src/payload.rs: BtreePayload abstraction for reading across page boundaries (local + overflow)
- crates/fsqlite-ast/src/lib.rs: Complete AST type hierarchy -- Statement, SelectStatement, InsertStatement, UpdateStatement, DeleteStatement, CreateTableStatement, Expr, JoinClause, OrderingTerm, WindowDefn, etc.
- crates/fsqlite-parser/src/lexer.rs: Token enum, memchr-accelerated scanning for string literals and comments, keyword classification
- crates/fsqlite-parser/src/parser.rs: Recursive descent with Pratt precedence for expressions, all statement types from Section 12
- crates/fsqlite-parser/src/keyword.rs: Perfect hash (or PHF crate) for 150+ SQL keywords with O(1) lookup

### Crates Involved
- fsqlite-btree (cursor, cell, balance, overflow, freelist, payload)
- fsqlite-ast (AST type hierarchy)
- fsqlite-parser (lexer, parser, keyword)

### LOC Estimate
~12,000 LOC (btree: 5,000, parser: 4,000, ast: 3,000)

### Entry Criteria (Dependencies)
- Phase 2 complete (B-tree depends on VFS for page I/O, parser depends on types for AST nodes)

### Exit Criteria (Acceptance)
- B-tree: Insert 10,000 random i64 keys, verify all retrievable via cursor
- B-tree: Insert 10,000 sequential keys, delete 5,000 random subset, verify remaining 5,000 present and in order
- B-tree: Insert keys that force overflow pages (payload > page_size/4), verify read-back
- B-tree: Insert/delete pattern that causes tree depth to increase to 3 and then decrease back to 2
- B-tree: Freelist correctly tracks freed pages and reuses them on insert
- B-tree: Proptest -- random mix of insert/delete/lookup operations, invariant: cursor iteration always returns keys in sorted order
- Parser: Parse all statement types from Section 12 (at least one test per subsection)
- Parser: Expression precedence: 1 + 2 * 3 parses as 1 + (2 * 3)
- Parser: All join types, CTE syntax, window function syntax
- Parser: Round-trip property test: parse -> pretty-print -> re-parse produces identical AST for 1000 generated SQL statements
- Parser: Error recovery: invalid SQL produces error with line:column span
- Parser: Keywords as identifiers in non-reserved positions (e.g., column named "order" in SELECT "order" FROM t)
- Target: 500+ tests

### Risk Areas
- B-tree balance is the most algorithmically complex code in SQLite. balance_nonroot alone is ~800 lines of C (lines 8230-9033 in btree.c). Incorrect balancing causes silent data corruption. Mitigation: extensive proptest with invariant checking after every operation (cell count, key ordering, child pointers, freespace accounting).
- Parser completeness: SQLite's grammar has many context-sensitive corners (e.g., REPLACE is both a keyword and a function name). Mitigation: use parse.y as the authoritative reference, test every production.

### Test Requirements
1. test_btree_insert_10k_random_keys: Insert 10,000 random i64 keys, verify all retrievable via cursor
2. test_btree_insert_delete_5k: Insert 10,000 sequential keys, delete 5,000 random subset, verify remaining 5,000 in sorted order
3. test_btree_overflow_pages: Insert keys forcing overflow pages (payload > page_size/4), verify read-back
4. test_btree_depth_increase_decrease: Insert/delete pattern causing tree depth increase to 3 then decrease to 2
5. test_btree_freelist_reclaim: Freelist correctly tracks freed pages and reuses them on insert
6. test_btree_proptest_order_invariant: proptest random insert/delete/lookup, invariant: cursor iteration always sorted
7. test_parser_all_statement_types: Parse all statement types from Section 12
8. test_parser_expression_precedence: 1 + 2 * 3 parses as 1 + (2 * 3)
9. test_parser_join_types: All join types (INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL, CROSS, NATURAL)
10. test_parser_cte_syntax: WITH clause, recursive CTE syntax
11. test_parser_window_function_syntax: OVER clause, PARTITION BY, ORDER BY, frame spec
12. test_parser_roundtrip_proptest: parse -> pretty-print -> re-parse produces identical AST for 1000 generated SQL
13. test_parser_error_recovery: Invalid SQL produces error with line:column span
14. test_parser_keywords_as_identifiers: Keywords in non-reserved positions
15. test_parser_keyword_lookup_o1: Perfect hash keyword lookup with all 150+ keywords

## Acceptance Criteria
- The deliverables and tests in this bead (see "Exit Criteria (Acceptance)" and "Test Requirements") are implemented and passing.
- Proptest + deterministic tests demonstrate no silent B-tree corruption under adversarial insert/delete patterns.

### Spec Content (Lines 15930-15995)

**Deliverables:**
- fsqlite-btree/cursor.rs: BtCursor with page-stack traversal (max depth 20 for 4KB pages, interior fanout ~300-400 for table B-trees)
- fsqlite-btree/cell.rs: Cell parsing for all 4 page types (INTKEY table leaf/interior, BLOBKEY index leaf/interior), overflow detection, local payload calculation
- fsqlite-btree/balance.rs: Page splitting -- balance_nonroot (redistribute cells among siblings, typically 3-way split), balance_deeper (root overflow, increase tree depth by 1)
- fsqlite-btree/overflow.rs: Overflow page chain read/write, chain link following, overflow page allocation from freelist
- fsqlite-btree/freelist.rs: Trunk + leaf freelist page management, allocation (prefer leaf pages from first trunk), deallocation
- fsqlite-btree/payload.rs: BtreePayload abstraction for reading across page boundaries (local + overflow)
- fsqlite-ast/lib.rs: Complete AST type hierarchy (Statement, SelectStatement, InsertStatement, UpdateStatement, DeleteStatement, CreateTableStatement, Expr, JoinClause, OrderingTerm, WindowDefn, etc.)
- fsqlite-parser/lexer.rs: Token enum, memchr-accelerated scanning for string literals and comments, keyword classification
- fsqlite-parser/parser.rs: Recursive descent with Pratt precedence for expressions, all statement types from Section 12
- fsqlite-parser/keyword.rs: Perfect hash (or PHF crate) for 150+ SQL keywords with O(1) lookup

**Dependencies:** Phase 2 complete (B-tree depends on VFS for page I/O, parser depends on types for AST nodes).

**Risk areas:** balance_nonroot (~800 lines of C, btree.c:8230-9033) is most algorithmically complex code. Incorrect balancing = silent data corruption. Mitigation: extensive proptest with invariant checking (cell count, key ordering, child pointers, freespace accounting). Parser: context-sensitive corners (e.g., REPLACE is keyword and function name). Mitigation: use parse.y as reference, test every production.

**Estimated complexity:** ~12,000 LOC (btree: 5,000, parser: 4,000, ast: 3,000). Target: 500+ tests.

### Unit Tests Required
1. test_btree_insert_10k_random_keys: Insert 10,000 random i64 keys, verify all retrievable via cursor
2. test_btree_insert_delete_5k: Insert 10,000 sequential keys, delete 5,000 random subset, verify remaining 5,000 present and in sorted order
3. test_btree_overflow_pages: Insert keys forcing overflow pages (payload > page_size/4), verify read-back
4. test_btree_depth_increase_decrease: Insert/delete pattern causing tree depth to increase to 3 then decrease back to 2
5. test_btree_freelist_reclaim: Freelist correctly tracks freed pages and reuses them on insert
6. test_btree_proptest_order_invariant: proptest random mix of insert/delete/lookup, invariant: cursor iteration always returns keys in sorted order (reference BTreeMap comparison)
7. test_btree_cursor_depth_4_traversal: Insert keys forcing tree depth to 4, verify cursor visits all keys
8. test_btree_overflow_chain_100kb: Overflow page chain for 100KB payload, read back complete
9. test_btree_freelist_accounting: Freelist reclaims pages, verify via dbstat-equivalent accounting
10. test_parser_all_statement_types: Parse all statement types from Section 12 (at least one test per subsection)
11. test_parser_expression_precedence: 1 + 2 * 3 parses as 1 + (2 * 3)
12. test_parser_join_types: All join types (INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL, CROSS, NATURAL)
13. test_parser_cte_syntax: WITH clause, recursive CTE syntax
14. test_parser_window_function_syntax: OVER clause, PARTITION BY, ORDER BY, frame spec
15. test_parser_roundtrip_proptest: parse -> pretty-print -> re-parse produces identical AST for 1000 generated SQL statements
16. test_parser_error_recovery: Invalid SQL produces error with line:column span
17. test_parser_keywords_as_identifiers: Keywords in non-reserved positions (column named "order" in SELECT "order" FROM t)
18. test_parser_keyword_lookup_o1: Perfect hash keyword lookup with all 150+ keywords

### E2E Test
End-to-end validation: Create an in-memory B-tree backed by MemoryVfs, insert 10,000 records with varying payload sizes (some triggering overflow), delete a random subset, verify cursor iteration returns all remaining keys in sorted order. Separately, parse a complex SQL statement containing joins, CTEs, window functions, and subqueries, pretty-print it, re-parse, and verify AST identity. Then combine: parse an INSERT statement, extract values from AST, insert them into the B-tree, parse a SELECT, and verify cursor retrieval matches expected results.

 {"id":"bd-2ly4","title":"E2E Real SQLite Demos: Compatibility, Concurrency Perf, Corruption Recovery","description":"## Objective
Build a set of **truly end-to-end** demo/harness tools that:

- Use **real SQLite database files** (sourced from `/dp`), but **NEVER modify originals**.
- Run **massively concurrent write workloads** using FrankenSQLite vs regular `sqlite3`.
- Prove correctness via:
  - **Logical equivalence** (query results + canonical dumps) AND
  - **Bit-for-bit identity** of a canonicalized final database image (`sha256` matches).
- Produce a **clear performance story** (throughput/latency vs concurrency) showing where FrankenSQLite wins.
- Demonstrate **corruption + recovery**: corrupt copies of DB files and show baseline SQLite can't self-heal, while FrankenSQLite tooling can repair using a “RaptorQ everywhere” mentality.

## Key Constraints (Non-Negotiable)
- **Golden safety:** all `/dp` DBs are treated as read-only inputs. Demos operate on copied working sets.
- **No secrets in git:** DB fixtures from `/dp` must be `.gitignore`’d; only manifests/metadata are committed.
- **Determinism:** workloads must be reproducible by seed; artifacts include manifests and operation logs.
- **Compatibility realism:** default path targets standard SQLite file-format workflows (not toy/in-memory only).

## Correctness Strategy (Two Tiers)
1. **Tier A: Semantic equivalence**
   - Compare result sets and canonical SQL dumps.
2. **Tier B: Byte-identical image**
   - Canonicalize final logical state into a deterministic DB image (e.g., via `VACUUM INTO` or dump+reimport) and require identical `sha256`.

Tier B gives a practical “bit-identical” gate even before FrankenSQLite’s file-backed path is fully identical in low-level allocation behavior.

## Deliverables (High Level)
- `sample_sqlite_db_files/` fixture staging area + manifest tooling
- Rust harness binaries and/or `e2e/` scripts:
  - Workload generation (OpLog)
  - Executors for `sqlite3` and FrankenSQLite
  - Canonicalization + sha256 verification
  - Perf measurement + report output
  - Corruption injector + RaptorQ repair tool
- Optional `frankentui`-powered TUI dashboard for demos

## Definition of Done for This Epic
- A single command can run a demo suite end-to-end on a local machine and produce:
  - a correctness report (including sha256 matches for canonical images)
  - a performance report (speedup curves)
  - a corruption/recovery report

(Individual tasks below are responsible for specifying exact commands and acceptance gates.)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-10T04:17:29.157592463Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T04:40:39.512164507Z","closed_at":"2026-02-10T04:40:39.512145912Z","close_reason":"Duplicate of existing E2E suite epic bd-z0xm/sub-epics; created during onboarding before noticing existing bead graph.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["demo","durability","e2e","perf","sqlite"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2ly4","depends_on_id":"bd-1dma","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-26T22:57:55Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2ly4","depends_on_id":"bd-1e26","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-26T22:57:55Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2ly4","depends_on_id":"bd-249o","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-26T22:57:55Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2ly4","depends_on_id":"bd-2cvv","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-26T22:57:55Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2ly4","depends_on_id":"bd-ak8j","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-26T22:57:55Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-2ly4","depends_on_id":"bd-ox09","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-26T22:57:55Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

Every field with offset, size, valid values, FrankenSQLite defaults:
- [0:16] Magic "SQLite format 3\0" (required)
- [16:2] Page size: 512-65536 powers of 2 (1 encodes 65536). Default 4096.
- [18:1] Write version: 1=journal, 2=WAL. Default 2.
- [19:1] Read version: 1=journal, 2=WAL. Default 2.
- [20:1] Reserved space/page: 0-255 (usable_size = page_size - reserved >= 480). Default 0 (16 if page_checksum=ON).
- [21:3] Payload fractions: 64/32/32 (fixed, MUST be exactly these values).
- [24:4] File change counter. Incremented on header write (rollback commit, checkpoint page 1). NOT forced every WAL commit.
- [28:4] DB size in pages (valid when offset 92 == offset 24; else compute from file size).
- [32:4] First freelist trunk (0 if empty). [36:4] Total freelist pages.
- [40:4] Schema cookie (incremented on schema change).
- [44:4] Schema format number: 1-4. Default 4.
- [48:4] Suggested cache size. [52:4] Largest root b-tree (auto-vacuum). [56:4] Text encoding: 1=UTF8 (default), 2=UTF16le, 3=UTF16be.
- [60:4] User version. [64:4] Incremental vacuum. [68:4] Application ID.
- [72:20] Reserved (zeros). [92:4] Version-valid-for (= change counter when header valid). [96:4] SQLite version: 3052000.

**Forward compat:** Read version > 2 -> SQLITE_CANTOPEN. Write version > 2 -> open read-only.

## §11.2 B-Tree Page Layout

Page structure: [Header 8/12B] [Cell ptr array 2*N_cells B] [Unallocated space] [Cell content area, grows backward] [Reserved space].

**Header fields:** type (0x02 index-interior, 0x05 table-interior, 0x0A index-leaf, 0x0D table-leaf), first freeblock offset (u16BE), num cells (u16BE), cell content start (u16BE, 0=65536), fragmented bytes, right-child pointer (interior only, 4B extra).

Page 1 special: 100-byte db header before page header. Cell offsets account for prefix.

**Freeblock list:** Deleted cells form linked list within cell content area (2B next ptr + 2B size, min 4B). Fragmented bytes (offset 7) counts 1-3 byte gaps. Max fragments 60 before defrag.

### §11.2.1 Varint Encoding

Custom Huffman-like, NOT protobuf/LEB128. Max 9 bytes. First 8 bytes: high bit set = continue (7 bits contribute). 9th byte contributes ALL 8 bits. Max value: full u64. Critical difference: 9 bytes for full 64-bit (vs protobuf's 10).

## §11.3 Cell Formats

**Table leaf (0x0D):** [payload_size:varint][rowid:varint][payload:bytes][overflow_pgno:u32BE if overflow]
**Table interior (0x05):** [left_child:u32BE][rowid:varint]
**Index leaf (0x0A):** [payload_size:varint][payload:bytes][overflow_pgno:u32BE if overflow]
**Index interior (0x02):** [left_child:u32BE][payload_size:varint][payload:bytes][overflow_pgno:u32BE if overflow]

## §11.4 Overflow Pages

usable = page_size - reserved. Table leaf: max_local = usable-35, min_local = (usable-12)*32/255-23. Index: max_local = (usable-12)*64/255-23, min_local same. If payload > max_local: local = min_local + (payload-min_local)%(usable-4); if local > max_local: local = min_local.

For 4096/0 reserved: table max=4061, index max=1002.

Overflow page: [next_pgno:u32BE][payload:usable-4 bytes].

## §11.5 Freelist

Trunk page: [next_trunk:u32BE][leaf_count:u32BE][leaf_pages:u32BE*K]. Max leaves = (usable-8)/4 = 1022 @4096.

## §11.6 Pointer Map (Auto-Vacuum)

5 bytes/entry: type code (1=ROOTPAGE, 2=FREEPAGE, 3=OVERFLOW1, 4=OVERFLOW2, 5=BTREE) + parent u32BE. First at page 2. entries_per_page = usable/5. Group = entries+1. For 4096: 819 entries, group 820, pages at 2, 822, 1642...

 {"id":"bd-2ma8","title":"§13.1 Core Scalar Functions: abs/hex/length/lower/upper/typeof/etc (All 60+ Functions)","description":"## SUMMARY
Implement all 60+ core scalar SQL functions from SQLite 3.52.0, including abs, char, coalesce, concat, concat_ws, format/printf, glob, hex, iif/if, ifnull, instr, last_insert_rowid, length, like, likelihood/likely/unlikely, lower, upper, ltrim/rtrim/trim, max (scalar), min (scalar), nullif, octet_length, quote, random, randomblob, replace, round, sign, soundex, substr/substring, typeof, subtype, unhex, unicode, unistr, zeroblob, sqlite_version, sqlite_source_id, sqlite_compileoption_used, sqlite_compileoption_get, changes, total_changes, and sqlite_offset. All functions follow SQLite's NULL propagation rule: if any argument is NULL the result is NULL, unless specifically documented otherwise (e.g., char skips NULLs, concat treats NULL as empty string, coalesce short-circuits on first non-NULL).

## KEY DATA STRUCTURES / ALGORITHMS
- FunctionRegistry: maps function name -> ScalarFunction trait object with arity, determinism flag, and optional encoding preference.
- ScalarFunction trait: fn invoke(&self, cx: &mut Cx, args: &[SqliteValue]) -> Result<SqliteValue>. Each function is a struct implementing this trait.
- SqliteValue enum: Null | Integer(i64) | Real(f64) | Text(String) | Blob(Vec<u8>). Functions must handle type coercion per SQLite affinity rules.
- For format/printf: custom formatter implementing %d, %f, %e/%E, %g/%G, %s, %q, %Q, %w, %c, %z, %% with width/precision/flags. %n is deliberately disabled (security).
- For like/glob: pattern matching with backtracking or NFA-based approach; like is case-insensitive for ASCII, glob is case-sensitive; optional ESCAPE character for like.
- For substr/substring: 1-based indexing, special handling for START=0 quirk (returns max(LENGTH-1,0) elements from start), negative START counts from end, negative LENGTH returns characters preceding START.
- Scalar max/min (multi-arg) vs aggregate max/min (single-arg): scalar form returns NULL if ANY argument is NULL; aggregate form ignores NULLs per SQL standard.
- round uses round-half-away-from-zero (NOT banker's rounding).
- abs of minimum i64 (-9223372036854775808) must raise integer overflow error.
- subtype does NOT propagate NULL: subtype(NULL) = 0.

## NORMATIVE INVARIANTS
1. NULL propagation: every function returns NULL when any argument is NULL, unless the function is specifically documented otherwise (char, concat, coalesce, ifnull, subtype, etc.).
2. abs(MIN_I64) MUST raise an integer overflow error, not return a negative value or wrap.
3. coalesce and iif MUST short-circuit evaluation (arguments after the result are not evaluated).
4. concat(NULL, 'x') = 'x' (NULL treated as empty), but 'x' || NULL = NULL (|| propagates NULL).
5. format %n MUST be a no-op (security: never writes to memory).
6. hex of a number: converts to text representation first, then hex-encodes UTF-8 bytes (NOT raw IEEE-754 bits).
7. last_insert_rowid: trigger-inserted rowids MUST NOT change the value observable after the outer statement completes.
8. length of text counts characters, not bytes; length of blob counts bytes.
9. Scalar max(X,Y,...) returns NULL if ANY argument is NULL; aggregate max(col) ignores NULLs.
10. round uses round-half-away-from-zero: round(2.5)=3.0, round(-2.5)=-3.0.
11. substr START=0 quirk must be implemented exactly as C SQLite does.
12. typeof returns exactly one of: "null", "integer", "real", "text", "blob".
13. sqlite_version SHOULD report the claimed SQLite feature compatibility target so application feature detection works.
14. octet_length returns byte count of UTF-8 encoding, differing from length for multi-byte characters.
15. unhex returns NULL for invalid hex (not an error), after removing characters specified in optional Y parameter.

All built-in functions follow SQLite's NULL propagation rule: if any argument is NULL, result is NULL, unless specifically documented otherwise.

**abs(X)** -> integer or real. Absolute value. If X is minimum i64 (-9223372036854775808), integer overflow error raised. Coerces numeric-looking strings.

**char(X1, X2, ..., XN)** -> text. String from Unicode code points. NULL args silently skipped.

**coalesce(X, Y, ...)** -> any. First non-NULL arg. Short-circuits.

**concat(X, Y, ...)** -> text (3.44+). Concatenates as text. NULL args treated as empty strings (unlike || which propagates NULL).

**concat_ws(SEP, X, Y, ...)** -> text (3.44+). Concatenates with separator. NULL args skipped entirely.

**format(FORMAT, ...)** / **printf(FORMAT, ...)** -> text. SQL printf with: %d (integer), %f (float, 6 decimals), %e/%E (scientific), %g/%G (shorter of f/e), %s (string, NULL->empty), %q (single-quotes doubled), %Q (like %q wrapped in quotes, NULL->"NULL"), %w (double-quotes for identifiers), %c (char from code point), %n (disabled no-op), %z (same as %s), %% (literal %). Width/precision/flags (-,+, ,0) supported.

**glob(PATTERN, STRING)** -> 0 or 1. Case-sensitive. * matches any sequence, ? single char, [...] character classes.

**hex(X)** -> text. Hex rendering. Blob: each byte -> 2 hex chars. Text: UTF-8 bytes hex-encoded. Number: first to text, then hex-encoded (NOT raw IEEE-754 bits).

**iif(B1, V1 [, B2, V2, ...] [, ELSE])** -> any. Multi-condition form (3.49+). Short-circuits. **if()** is alias (3.48+). Two-argument iif(COND, X) returns NULL when false (3.48+).

**ifnull(X, Y)** -> any. X if non-NULL, else Y. Equivalent to coalesce(X, Y).

**instr(X, Y)** -> integer. 1-based position of first Y in X, 0 if not found. Blob: bytes. Text: characters.

**last_insert_rowid()** -> integer. Most recent INSERT rowid on same connection. Trigger inserts MUST NOT change the value observable after outer statement completes.

**length(X)** -> integer. Text: character count. Blob: byte count. NULL: NULL. Number: length of text representation.

**like(PATTERN, STRING [, ESCAPE])** -> integer. Case-insensitive. % any sequence, _ single char.

**likelihood(X, P)** -> any. Returns X unchanged. Planner hint with probability P (0.0-1.0, compile-time constant).

**likely(X)** -> any. Equivalent to likelihood(X, 0.9375).

**unlikely(X)** -> any. Equivalent to likelihood(X, 0.0625).

**lower(X)** -> text. ASCII lowercase only (ICU needed for full Unicode).

**upper(X)** -> text. ASCII uppercase only.

**ltrim(X [, Y])**, **rtrim(X [, Y])**, **trim(X [, Y])** -> text. Remove chars in Y (default spaces) from left/right/both sides.

**max(X, Y, ...)** (scalar) -> any. Maximum value. If ANY arg is NULL, returns NULL immediately. (Aggregate max() ignores NULLs.)

**min(X, Y, ...)** (scalar) -> any. Minimum value. Same NULL semantics as scalar max().

**nullif(X, Y)** -> any. NULL if X = Y, else X.

**octet_length(X)** -> integer (3.43+). Bytes in UTF-8 encoding. Numbers first converted to text. Equivalent to length(CAST(X AS BLOB)).

**quote(X)** -> text. SQL-safe rendering. Text: single-quoted. Blobs: X'hex'. NULL: "NULL". Numbers: as-is.

**random()** -> integer. Pseudo-random 64-bit signed integer.

**randomblob(N)** -> blob. N bytes of pseudo-random data.

**replace(X, Y, Z)** -> text. Every Y in X replaced with Z. Empty Y returns X unchanged.

**round(X [, N])** -> real. Round half away from zero (NOT banker's rounding). Default N=0.

**sign(X)** -> integer. -1, 0, +1. NULL for NULL. NULL for non-numeric strings.

**soundex(X)** -> text. 4-char Soundex. ?000 for empty/NULL.

**substr(X, START [, LENGTH])** / **substring()**: 1-based for START>0. START=0 quirk: with LENGTH>0, returns max(LENGTH-1,0) from start; without LENGTH, behaves like START=1. Negative START counts from end. Negative LENGTH returns abs(LENGTH) chars preceding START.

**typeof(X)** -> "null", "integer", "real", "text", or "blob".

**subtype(X)** -> integer. Subtype tag. Does NOT propagate NULL: subtype(NULL) = 0.

**unhex(X [, Y])** -> blob (3.41+). Decodes hex. Y specifies ignorable chars. NULL if invalid hex.

**unicode(X)** -> integer. Code point of first character.

**unistr(X)** -> text (3.45+). Interprets \uXXXX and \UXXXXXXXX escapes.

**zeroblob(N)** -> blob. N zero bytes, efficiently represented internally.

**sqlite_version()** -> text. Returns "3.52.0" (or compatible version string).

**sqlite_source_id()** -> text. Source identification string.

**sqlite_compileoption_used(X)** -> 0 or 1. Checks if compile option was used.

**sqlite_compileoption_get(N)** -> text or NULL. Nth compile-time option.

**changes()** -> integer. Rows modified by most recent DML.

**total_changes()** -> integer. Total rows modified since connection opened.

**sqlite_offset(X)** -> integer. Byte offset of column value within record payload. Only for direct column references.
